questions.question:
Answer with the option letter only. Which column order do the visible portico columns most closely resemble?
A. Doric
B. Ionic
C. Corinthian
D. Tuscan
Answer with yes or no only. Is the triangular pediment visible in this image?
What does the Latin inscription above the columns of the landmark in the image mean?
questions.expected: It states that Marcus Agrippa, son of Lucius, built it when he was consul for the third time.
questions.rgb_answer: The Latin inscription above the columns of the landmark reads "MAGRIFFAL L.F. COSTERTIVM FECIT," which translates to "Magrippa, son of Lucius, made this." This indicates that the structure was built or sponsored by a person named Magrippa, who was likely a son of someone named Lucius. The term "FECIT" is a common Latin verb meaning "made" or "built," suggesting that Magrippa was responsible for the construction or dedication of the building.

gt_answers.Small: The Latin inscription above the columns of the landmark in the image reads 'MAGRIFFIUS COSECT'.
gt_answers.Medium: The Latin inscription above the columns of the landmark in the image reads 'MAGRIPPA COSEPT,' which translates to 'Magrippa built.'
gt_answers.Large: The Latin inscription above the columns of the landmark reads "MAGRIPPA L. F. COS TERTIUM," which translates to "Magrippa, son of Lucius Furius, Consul for the third time." This indicates that the building was likely dedicated to or constructed by a person named Lucius Furius Magrippa, who held the office of Consul for the third time, suggesting he was a prominent figure in Roman politics during his tenure.

rendered_answers.Small: The Latin inscription above the columns of the landmark in the image reads "MAGISTERIUM PRINCEPS," which translates to "Master Prince."
rendered_answers.Medium: The Latin inscription above the columns of the landmark in the image reads "MAGNAE GRACIAE," which translates to "Great Favor" or "Great Favor of the Graces."
rendered_answers.Large: The Latin inscription above the columns of the landmark in the image reads "MAGISTERIUM" and "COSTRUTTORE," which translates to "Masterpiece" and "Builder."
